channel.title: Transcripts: Glenn Diesen
channel.description: YouTube video transcripts from Glenn Diesen

item.title: Jiang Xueqin: The Iran War & the Battle for the Petrodollar
item.description:
Jiang Xueqin discusses how the Iran War represent a war over the petrodollar. Jiang is the host of the popular educational channel Predictive History: https://www.youtube.com/@PredictiveHistory
Follow Prof. Glenn Diesen: Substack: https://glenndiesen.substack.com/ X/Twitter: https://x.com/Glenn_Diesen Patreon: https://www.patreon.com/glenndiesen
Support the research by Prof. Glenn Diesen: PayPal: https://www.paypal.com/paypalme/glenndiesen Buy me a Coffee: buymeacoffee.com/gdieseng Go Fund Me: https://gofund.me/09ea012f
Books by Prof. Glenn Diesen: https://www.amazon.com/stores/author/B09FPQ4MDL
item.content:encoded:
YouTube: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=P_DHMUdOVdo

Transcript

Welcome back. We are joined today by Jang Shu Shin uh to discuss the developments uh between the United States and uh Iran. Uh so thank you very much for coming back on. >> Thanks. >> So I I well the first thing I think about is in regards to this war is that the Iranian targets all seem to be very well calculated in terms of economics. And I think this is interesting because usually in all American wars they there's some economic interests to go to

war but now we see them um well not just shutting down the straight of moose but you see them when they essentially forced the Americans to spend their expensive uh interceptive missiles. All of this has seems to have an economic underpinning but the really big thing appears to be the possible attack now on the petro dollar. I was wondering how do you see how do you see the vulnerability of the petro dollar in this war? >> Right. So I would argue that right now this war in Iran it's primarily about maintaining the petro dollar because

these past 10 years the world has become increasingly concerned about the viability of the petro dollar. Um right now the United States is $39 trillion in debt. Um the United States uh right after the Russians um invaded Ukraine froze over $200 billion dollars in Russian assets which makes the world question the legitimacy as well as the credibility of the US financial system because if the Americans can weaponize the dollar against the Russians they can

weaponize US dollar against everyone. So after that you saw this massive exodus from US treasuries um and right now China is establishing the um gold corridor. Um so brakes is trying to create alternatives to the petrod dollar but for the Americans the petro dollar is the very basis of their empire and quite honestly it's the very uh it's it's the objective of their empire. Um so the United States right now is going to fight tooth and nail to protect the petro dollar. What this basically means is to use its naval

supremacy, its control over strategic choke points uh maritime choke points to control trade access and basically force nations to depend on US resources. So we saw that United States um in January um attacked Venezuela and now it's attacked Iran. Um recently uh these past few days there was a Ukrainian drone strike on uh Russia's main export uh hub which basically took off 40% of Russian oil

from the global market. And so Europe and East Asia are now forced to depend on the United States, North America for energy. Um, and right now we're seeing a uh pivot uh uh from Europe and East Asia over to North America. And so that's a strategy in place where uh the United States basically wants to take take off uh Russian um GC oil and force the world to buy from the United States which protects the petro dollar. But what we

know from history is that this sort of hubris will lead to um a backlash and it will lead to the world unifying against uh the America eventually because quite honestly America used to be the policeman of the world and that's become the pirate of the world and so uh maybe in the short term you will see the strategy working out but definitely in the long term you will see the world becoming much more aggravated by this um piracy. So what do they need to achieve then uh in in this war? Because uh well I was

wondering if you were surprised that uh the Americans appear to have walked away from the negotiations at least that's what it seemed like in uh Islamabad. Uh do do you is is this to continue the war then or is it simply because they can't well the petro dollar won't survive if the straight over moose is with Iranians or what is the thinking here? >> Right. So we've seen a very strange sequence of events these past six weeks. Uh the war has been going on for about six weeks and we've seen a lot of

strange event events. So the moment that the Americans attacked Tran and killed uh their supreme leader, the Iranians uh basically closed off the shumoose. And in response, what the Americans did, which was very surprising, is they basically removed sanctions on both Russian and Iranian oil, allowing the Russians and Iranians to um make a lot of money selling the oil. So that was sort of surprising. And Scott Bezen, the the Treasury Secretary, explained that this is to maintain global stability. um he's afraid that um oil will reach $200

and this would destabilize the global economy. And as this war progressed um we saw the United States escalate to attacking civilian infrastructure like universities um bridges and railways. In fact, there's real concern that the Americans and Israelis plan to um choke and strangle Tran to death, meaning cutting off the railway system, the roads, uh which would um put Tran under siege. They couldn't get food and water from the other parts of Iran. And Tran, a city of 10 million people, would be in a

lot of trouble if that that were the case. So, what we were seeing was constant escalation. Then last week uh we were on the brink because Trump basically declared that if Iran did not open the shoo then the Americans would bomb Iran back to the stone age and Iran obviously would would retaliate by destroying GCC energy infrastructure and the dissellation plants. So last Tuesday we were really on the brink and it seemed as though um we were heading towards war three. Uh but then Trump did something completely surprising which is

he basically uh offered an unconditional surrender. Iran had a 10-point plan which included uh that Iran would be allowed to enrich uranium. It also included that all economic sanctions against Iran would be lifted. Iran would be given sovereignty over the Hummus and all security guarantees would be applied to both Iran and its proxies, the Hu, Hezbollah, Hamas. So these were the um 10 points um that Iran demanded and Americans um Donald Trump basically said that this is a workable framework and so they agreed to meet in Sabbad to discuss

these 10 points um and use them as a framework for a possible long-term ceasefire. We know that once they reach um Isabad uh JD Vance uh Jared Kushner and Cricov basically blew off the Iranians. They were not serious about negotiations. Um the Americans went in uh with some goodwill um hoping to reach an agreement and that and that's where the Iranians went, but the Americans just just basically said no, we're we're going to stick to our original demands. And so the negotiations went nowhere. And so now we're back um to where we

were uh a week ago. So the big question then is why would the Americans do this? What what's the point of this ceasefire? And so um we can only speculate here. Okay. So, so, so let me try to figure out how Donald Trump thinks because he's a businessman. And what does he want? Well, his main objective is to maintain American imperial supremacy u by maintaining the petro dollar. And that basically means forcing China and Japan and South Korea um and Europe to buy energy using the US dollar. That's what

the petro dollar literally means. And so maybe the point of this war was to destroy the GCC and Iran's capacity to deliver oil to the world market and force the world to rely on North America, Canada, Mexico, and the United States. Okay? So let's just say that is the main objective. And obviously Trump can't announce this because this would piss off the entire world and you have the entire world against the United States. Well, in order for him to pull this off, he would basically need to gauge China's reaction because we know

that China and Iran are very strong allies. China has invested a great deal in Iranian infrastructure as part of the Bel and Road initiative and China buys a lot of oil from Iran. In fact, China buys about 90% of all Iranian oil. Um, so basically Trump needs to figure out what can I pull this off and would China agree to buy oil from me? Okay, so he's a negotiator. He's his businessman. He's practical. He's trying to figure out what the um pressure points on China are. And I would make the argument that this u Iran Iran agreeing to this is

some of negotiations would not have been possible if China did not apply pressure on Iran. Okay. I would make this argument because the Iranians have been negotiating with Americans these past few months and they know that the Americans use these negotiations as a pretext in order to um assassinate leaders in order to um do surprise attacks. So the Iranians know the Americans have absolutely no credibility at all. The Americans are not serious at all about peace negotiations. And these past few weeks, the Iranians

have been adamant saying, "You start this war. Um, we're just protecting our sovereignty and there's some important negotiations. Let's just fight this to the better end." I think that was the right approach. This is what you this is the attitude you must take against bullies. So why is it that um the Iranians suddenly had a change of heart and says fine, let's negotiate in Islamabad. In fact, they set a pretty they sent a pretty high level delegation to Islamabad, including the foreign minister um to partake in these negotiations knowing that Israel has a history of assassinating uh negotiation teams. Okay. So, they put their diplomats at tremendous risk and I think

the only explanation is that right now the Chinese economy is under a lot of strain. China receives about 60% of its oil and energy from the Middle East including Qatar and Saudi Arabia. um with China receives about a third of its energy needs from Venezuela, Russia and Iran combined and China's been very lucky in that these three places have been sanctioned. So China was has been able to receive these these the oil at a very deep discount uh which has really helped the Chinese uh economy and now um

and now a lot of this energy is being is moving offline. So the Chinese economy is a lot under a lot of stress. So Trump's trying to figure out, can I when I go to China in midMay, can I propose to Beijing, hey man, let's have a grand alliance where you start to buy energy from North America. And he's trying to figure that out. And given that Iran agreed to negotiations, I think that he's figured out that the answer is yes. China must agree because of the state of the economy. And this is why we saw right after the negotiations were

cancelled, Trump announced a naval blockade against Iran because he knows that right now China's a lot of strain and he can bring China into um into an agreement and and thus protect the petro dollar. So So that's my explanation for what's going on. I could be wrong. It's all speculation, but I think this is what makes the most sense so far. the naval blockade. This is a well a strange um conception because it's it's unclear how it's going to be upheld because they're not naturally in the straight either. They will be far

away. So it's essentially the blockade is would it entail attacking Chinese ships entering? I mean it seems uh absurd. What if they would um the Iranians open up for Indian ships? Are they going to start attacking Indian ships? it it doesn't really make that much sense. How how do you see this? What what is uh how would this uh blockade actually function or is it just meant to choke off the the Iranian ships? Right. So from a practical perspective,

it's actually very hard to implement because if you go close to the Iranian coastline to Hamus, then you're in range of Iranian ballistic missiles. And so um the Americans won't risk that because we we've seen that their aircraft carriers including the Gerald Ford neighbor and Lincoln have intentionally stayed away far away from um Iran's ballistic missile range. So they're afraid of ballistic missiles and they know that these ballistic missiles can sink their aircraft carriers which will be sorry which will be a tremendous tremendous uh defeat uh strategically for the

Americans. So I think this is a pretext to choke off the straight of Malaa. Right. So there are two major choke points uh for East Asia to receive oil from the um Middle East. The straight of Humus is what Iran controls. And so response, America plans to choke off the straight of um Mala, which is right now basically controlled by Indonesia and Malaysia, both of which are uh American military allies. And um these these nations have been heavily infiltrated by the CIA. So I think that's the grand strategy to basically use a straight malaka to choke off East Asia from um

from GCC energy. >> Yeah. But if the Americans actually go through with this uh attempt to blockade the the Iranians would probably respond with their ally in Yemen, that is to close off the Red Sea at uh Bab al-Mandab. So, I'm just it it seems that this could be well, it just seems like this plan is weak at many levels. But it kind of begs the question of how what do you see being the likely success of the United States achieving its objective uh not just with the blockade but also with

uh preserving the petro dollar because Iran is quite resilient and uh I'm assuming that the Chinese uh you know much like with the war against Russia that they they know that they could be next on the chopping block or that they at least are the ultimate targets. >> Right? So what America's mostly interested in is trying to sustain its debt. Right now America has $39 trillion in debt. That's not that's not that's that's not a problem as long as nations continue to buy US treasuries. And we've seen these past few years a movement away from US Treasury and more and more

towards gold. Um the Chinese especially are moving towards um gold. So how do you force East Asia and Europe to continue to buy US treasuries? Well, if you are the own supplier of energy of oil and gas, LG, they have absolutely no choice but to sell their gold and buy US treasuries in order to sustain their economies. Right. So, yes, you're absolutely right in that the Iranians have um counter measures. The Iranians could use the Houthies to close off the Red Sea. Uh they could attack um the

pipeline that connects Saudi Arabia to uh the Red Sea. There's many many uh measures that the Iranians can do, but the influence of the Iranians is contained to the Middle East. Remember, the ultimate objective is to knock off Middle East oil and energy from the market. And um that is very easy to do as long as as long as you continue to provoke this war. Now, I think the next step is a ground invasion in order to force Iranians to attack um GCC energy and dalination plants. Right? So the

entire goal is to destroy the Middle East which will force the world to pivot to North America. So in a long in in a short term this strategy will work out because there's absolutely no alternative uh for the world to um the Middle East energy but in the long term this will create a great deal of animosity um towards the United States which will ultimately destroy their empire. Well, Romano Prod, the former prime minister of Italy and also yeah, former president of the European Commission, he

he said something interesting along those lines. He was making the point that uh well, at some point the Europeans will have to start uh getting energy again from the Russians if they want to thrive. But he also made a prediction that the energy would open once the Americans would begin to control the energy infrastructure between Russia and Europe. So essentially the Americans taking over Nordstream. Uh this is when the energy will start flowing again. Uh which is um uh well an assumption I guess that uh that that that Americans want to be in control over the energy architecture

otherwise they will not permit it. Um but but but beyond this you know what what Trump might be planning a common argument is often that Trump is not necessarily fully in control that he's uh all uh if not compromised at least under great influence from various interests be it the neocons or the Israelis how do you see this the extent of uh Trump's control because um well given how different his second administration is compared to the first one often gets the impression that it

doesn't always matter who you know sits on the throne in the US. >> I think in the first administration there was a lot of concern um within the deep state and the neocons and um the different political factions within Washington DC about Trump's um capacity to be a leader because he was outrageous in his speech and his rhetoric. Uh the Europeans very much concerned about Trump. Trump started this trade war with China that was hurting the global economy um and it was hurting American

consumers. Uh Trump did not seem like a viable leader and he did not seem as though he could maintain the uh empire and people felt that if he stayed in office longer the Europeans would drift away. Um China would drift away. The entire world would drift would drift would drift away and American generally would would die. But then what happened of course was that Biden came to power and Biden was essentially comeomaos. He did nothing for four years. So Putin uh invaded Ukraine and Americans didn't

really have a forceful response. I mean they did do a lot of things like blow up the North Pipeline. They sanctioned uh Russian energy. They removed Russia from the um Swift system. They s they um froze $20 billion in Russian assets. They did a lot of things, but none of these things were actually uh useful. And the Empire decided that regardless of what we feel about Trump, um he is forceful. He will act whereas Biden and the Democrats may not act because essentially they're a bunch of

And so the decision was made to um reinstate Trump in 2024. And that's why we are here today. So Trump is an agent of empire. He is doing what the empire requires. Okay. And so what what happened before um during the unipolar moment basically from 1999 up to today was that America had a lot of goodwill and um it was able to achieve the consent of the world mainly by co-opting the global elite. Right? So um all these elites in Europe, in Russia, in China,

they're remaining concerned about um engaging as much corruption as possible so they can um basically transfer their illotten uh gains into America. And this doesn't work really well for the elite for a long long time. But obviously these nation states were not were not very happy with the state affairs. That's why you saw the rise of Putin in Russia and the rise of sea in China because there was a lot of concern among the nationalists that uh America was hauling out these these countries and they were completely right in the matter. So so now you're seeing a a

direct challenge to America empire and so the only challenge is you don't have the consent of these nation states then you must use force to um enforce your empire um w within these nation states. And so before the uh American military was used in order to guarantee peace in the past american Americana it was used to guarantee international trade. They were the policemen of the world in order to maintain the rules based national order. And now that's and now that Russia and China and other nations are questioning the legitimacy of the rules

based national order. What Trump is doing is he is transforming the military, the American military from a police force into a pirate force in order to extract tolls from uh the world. So we are in this situation because Trump's first strategy was to use tariffs um on the world, right? To force Europeans to pay tariffs to the American empire. But the Supreme Supreme Court struck that down because they said that the executive branch does not have the authority to um enforce tariffs. Only Congress does. So Trump's like, "Well,

you know what? If Cong if you won't let me enforce tariffs, then I'll just enforce tolls. Okay? I'll make the world pay tolls in order to have trade around the world." And and that's why we're seeing um um the Americans deploy one of their navy into the Caribbean to block trade into the Western Hemisphere. That's why we're seeing this war in Iran to block off um the world from uh Middle East energy. And that's why we're seeing right now the the US Navy uh basically boarding uh Russian shadow fleet

tankers. basically stealing Shadow Fleet tankers and and we'll see more of this as uh the months go by. >> Yeah. What's interesting with Trump though, there's some consistency here. I mean, many people I guess mistook his rhetoric for being um you know, all about peace. But if you listen to what he said all the way going back to the 1980s, he was essentially scolding uh all the Japanese and others for uh for you know, taking advantage of the US the way he saw it. So he he didn't say that we shouldn't have a big empire. He just said the empire uh they should pay us

for the privilege of being protected. So it's not that he didn't want an empire. It seemed he just wanted a better return on investment. That is you know you have to monetize the empire some otherwise the the empire will kill off the republic. So in other words it should feed it. It looks as this is where he's um his main differences. But that means not just extracting from adversaries but uh also allies. Um but uh I don't think it's limited to Trump. I think overall the United States will tilt more and more in this direction because as they

have as you said exhausted themselves with $39 trillion in the hole. Uh this it's not as if you can vote for a Democrat and suddenly they go back to paying for the security of allies anymore and having generous trade agreements. that there's no going back after you know exhausted to this extent. Sorry. >> Yeah. So this is a really important point that you make. Okay. So remember in 2016 when Trump first came out of office he was saying the same things uh you're saying which is everyone say take advantage of us especially China. So let's impose tariffs and make sure that um that it's America first that America

is winning in this um in this trade relationship. But after Trump left office, Biden came in with the Democrats and they institutionalized his policies. Okay? So then the first Trump administration, a lot of it was personal uh especially his tariff war against China and then Biden the Democrats made it institutional. And so we can the same um process here where maybe Trump leaves office in 2028, the Republicans lose, the Democrats come into office and they won't change anything. All they're going to do is institutionalize it and make it a

long-term sustainable policy because this is what benefits the American empire. So how will the great powers respond then? Because the US obviously shifting strategy. It's becoming more extractive and more aggressive. Again, it's not uh going well, it's not going down without a fight. How do you see the the Chinese and the Russians uh uh well reacting to this? you mentioned before the China's is gold corridor in terms of shifting

more to yeah gold instead of uh being reliant on the US dollar but uh what is seeing the overall all strategy here because my impression is at least from the Russians that they uh the interpretation that Trump was someone they could uh make peace with and uh you know reset relation it appears to be backsliding. I think they were quite appalled by what has happened to Iran, not just the attack on economic infrastructure but also the you know the killing of its leaders and it seems um yeah very much to go against their

interest and also Trump never actually did what he promised which was to end the war in Ukraine. In all fairness this could have been done if he would have cut off uh you know the intelligence agencies still working there. >> All right. So again, I think we give too much credit to Trump. I think that this is a natural response um of empire to its decline. Um we historically empires have never have never gone quietly into the night. They have flailed against uh the wind and they have tried to destroy the world as

they decline. And so we can see much more hubris. We will see much more thugy and piracy from America with or without Trump. And so I think we are in a situation um because of the limitations of Chinese strategic thinking. So um when China built the built and road initiative which was proper with which was a good strategy of trying to secure uh resource independence from the United States the problem was that when these Chinese ships went to collect commodities from overseas they were es escorted by the US Navy. When they came

back they were escorted by the US Navy. It was the US Navy that guaranteed the protection of Chinese trade. And it never occurred to Chinese policy makers that one day, one of these days, America could be like, why don't you steal the cargo? You know, why are we escorting these Chinese ships around the world? Why don't you steal it? And so, it never really occurred to Chinese policymakers all this possibility. And I don't know why why this is the case, but um we we can go into many possibilities, but the

re the reality is that China is now in a lot of trouble where China has invested $200 billion into the Middle East in infrastructure. China right now imports 50 to 50 to 60% of its oil needs from the Middle East. Now that the sh of moves um has been closed off now that this war is raging in the Middle East, China is in a lot of trouble. And again again this is all has to do with the limitations of Chinese strategic thinking. And so now China is in a lot of trouble and in the short

term China has absolutely no choice absolutely no choice at all but to agree to Trump's demands which is that China will become uh a major purchaser of America American LNG. China has absolutely no choice in the matter. Okay. Absolutely no choice. There's no there's China China can do know in the mid and long term what's going to happen is that Russia will start to challenge America in the seas. It's going to challenge America, American maritime naval supremacy. Basically, what what Russia is going to do is it's going to arm it shuttle fleet. And there's no way

that that Russia could defeat um America militarily uh in the seas. But America will be forced to fight a war of attrition. And over time, the American Navy will be downgraded. It will be degraded. and it'll be very hard for the American um Navy to replenish its lost ships. And so that's what what we're seeing in the long term. But in in the short term, there's actually nothing Russia and China can can do. Yeah. I often think about the current situation. It resembles a little bit

like before World War I. That is you saw Germany building and becoming stronger and stronger uh in terms of industrial production. Yeah. Steel production essentially all the key measurements. Meanwhile, the British control the seas. The entire their ability to actually compete against the British was uh uh you know was in the hand of the hands of the British. So all they had to do is begin to cut off or threaten their supply lines which is why the Germans also had to find you know uh other other possible corridors either land or sea. uh but it's a little bit with the

Chinese I feel they have all this well probably one of the motivations behind the belt and road initiative that is you can't rival the US as the economic leading economy while the Americans are then organizing their or organizing their supply lines and their their physical transportation corridors it doesn't make much sense uh but this return to uh I guess uh well piracy as you say or choking off these different transportation corridors We've seen, of course, the blockade on Venezuela, Cuba, the hijacking of these Russian ships. By

the way, the hijacking of Iranian tankers have happened for some years now before uh this war as well. Uh so so I I get it that hegeimon in decline will begin to weaponize all the economic levers of power that access to its technologies and industries, transportation corridors, banks, currencies, all of this is weaponized. Uh but the United States isn't all powerful either. I mean how what is the main challenge for the US to hold out because as you said they're struggling with their own debt. This is uh all their economic trouble is also

translating into social and political instability. So it looks as if you know they can't afford to play this game with the Iranians for too long. Uh that you know at some point they will have to put an end to this thing. Um, I think like the goal is to have this war continue for as long as possible. And the reason why is that if there were peace in the Middle East, what would happen is that Russia, Iran, and China would get together and form a trade alliance to counter America's

control over the seas. And this trade alliance could extend easily into Europe, into Africa. This is what what McKenna calls a heartland thesis, right? where America and Britain um the way for them to maintain their empire is by controlling uh maritime navigation and the great concern is that a power would emerge in Eurasia to unite the heartland and basically uh negate uh Anglo-American naval power and so this war in Iran cannot stop. If it stops then Russia could achieve its north

south uh trade corridor and China could implement its belt and road uh initiative and given the uh how America is starting to behave then I think that Europe um the Middle East Africa would happily join this trade alliance and basically um try to try to ignore the western hemisphere and Britain and so there's no way that Brit that America will seed Iran uh and and leave Iran alone. it's going to send in ground troops to create as much chaos as possible um and try to disrupt the bel road initiative. So what we're seeing is

that the Israelis and Americans are targeting railways uh in Iran which is crucial to the belt and road initiative and so we can we will we can expect um maybe more than the lawn of bel uh infrastructure uh for years and years. Yeah, I couldn't help but to notice the targeting of the railroads as well as well as some ports in the Caspian. So this entire international north south transportation corridor from uh which have been building from Russia, Iran to India, this is being uh undermined but also of course uh Iran's connectivity

with with China. So, um, but, uh, but I'm just wondering about the physibility here because, uh, how what exactly can boots on the ground achieve, but also can the United States get the boots on the ground because they're quite, uh, overextended already, >> right? So, um, the boots on on the ground, it's basically to maintain the war, right? So the problem with um with aerero warfare, which is what the Americans and Israel have been using for

the past six weeks, is that um your your it's going to be very hard to maintain your your air power. Uh we're already seeing some planes crashing because they're not wellmaintained. We're seeing the Iranians adapt very uh creatively um and resiliently to American air power. So you you can only establish aerial supremacy by having boots on the ground. And so I think the main objective of of the boots on the ground is not necessarily to take over Iran. I mean that's a suicide mission, but it's

basically to secure the coastline and try to degrade Iran's um air defenses and basically force um Iran on the defensive. The main objective I think if if if I'm fighting this war I think my main objective is to choke off tan basically to cut off all railways cut off all roads and basically force the population into starvation. >> So this is where you think we're going? Uh >> I think so. >> Destroying the population or as Trump would say kill off a civilization. >> That's right. That that that would be

the strategy moving forward. Yes. to to basically besiege Tran. >> Just such a big country, it seems hard to Yeah. Again, if the railroads are undermined, um but uh it also has his domestic problems though, the that Trump needs to, you know, it's not a popular war among the public and he has some fierce political opposition only intensified with the political polarization. Uh I I I did want to ask about NATO though because this is uh was

often perplexing to many Europeans because uh while you know in the Gulf States they're now discussing the extent to which uh the you know being frontline states for the US is actually harmful to the security. Uh they're having some of these talks in East Asia as well. But in Europe I mean they must be the most loyal obedient allies or frontline states of the United States. Uh I I'm I'm convinced they would have possibly joined the war on Iran if he would have asked them before it went south. But um but but why why is does it seem to be such an objective for

Trump to I guess uh undermine NATO because this is the main I mean the Europeans don't have much political imagination to have security without the United States. So, you know, it looks like a massive resource to have the Europeans essentially buy whatever America wants uh to to use, you know, use their currency, buy their energy, buy their expensive weapons. I mean, the Europeans will do anything if they believe America will be there for them. So, why make this big show out of we're not going to be there for you anymore? Cuz if this one thing that would make the Europeans break off uh and seek an

independent path from the United States, it's essentially the US has to shut the door on them because they're not otherwise they wouldn't shut the door on America. So, it's a bit strange how I can understand that NATO's not um that the Europeans, they're not really a force amplifier. They're not bringing much to the table. They're a bit of a cost, but still, it's a bit yeah, strange how dismissive he is of them, though. >> So, I think that the real war is actually not between United States and Iran. I mean, the real war is between the globalists and the nationalists, right? Right. So in the United States,

it it'd be a war between uh the financial elite uh as as rep represented by the city of London and Wall Street versus maggot America first. And so what Trump wants to do is basically transition America from an empire into something called a technate. Um which is the idea of a greater North America, right? So move away from the world and just focus on consolidating uh greater North America, taking over Canada, taking over Greenland, taking over Mexico, taking over Cuba, um Venezuela,

Colombia, uh Honduras, Nicawaga. Okay. So, so these countries that Trump has been pissing off for the past few months. Okay. But the grand vision is to transition America into greater than North America and become a continental fortress that sells resources to the rest of the world because the rest of the world is at war and in and is in desperate need of both fertilizer and energy. And so um Trump really sees NATO as part of the deep state as part of the global globalist class. And so he's

using this war as a pretext to sever relations with NATO. Basically, Trump would be perfectly happy if NATO were to go into Ukraine and be absolutely massacred by the Russians. That would solve um a major headache of of his. So, um yes, there is this war going on between United States and Iran, but we have we have to forget that there's but we should not forget that there's also a shadow war going on between um the globalists and the nationalists in uh America. This is also why Washington DC is supportive of Trump's war because the

globalists want Iran to be humble. They want Iran to be destroyed so that they can extend the hegemony of the empire. They think that Trump is a useful idiot, a puppet in uh in order to achieve this goal. And that's why the Democrats have not stopped him. You know there was recently two weeks ago no kings protest United States millions of people and like you you you were allowed to say no kings but you were not allowed to say no war right you would think like that people would be much more governized by an anti-war settlement than it is than

they are are by like an anti-tyrant settlement but during the protests which were organized by uh democratic lobbyist lobby groups uh which were very uh pro- Zionist you you are not allowed to voice any anti-war settlement. So it just goes to show you that all of Washington DC is fully behind this war and they think that Trump will be the scapegoat that that even if this war goes badly, Trump will be the one that takes all the blame. the Republicans will will be wiped out in the midterms and then uh

Democrats can uh steamroll back in office in 2028 and then they can implement a globalist agenda and destroy MAGA once and for all. >> Yeah, this language though the globalists versus the nationalists. It's fascinating that uh the first time I read about this divide was uh by Samuel Huntington none other back in 2004. He wrote this article dead souls where he was making the point that uh all this globalization had essentially created a a political elite detached from the

nation. So he was making the prediction uh that the future divisions in the world would be between the cosmopolitans or globalist versus the nationalists or patriots. So, uh, I thought it was insightful. Again, it's, uh, 2004. Yeah, it's been 22 years. And if you listen to, uh, a lot of the new populist leaders we have, this is what they're, yeah, Marin Le Pen to De, this is essentially the same language they also use. Now, uh, let me just ask a last question though. How does Russia fit into this? because all the pieces kind of make sense. The I understand the

rivalry between the United States, China, uh the the problem of Iran, but uh but Russia is, you know, it's an energy superpower in many ways. It's not doesn't have to be an opponent of the United States. Uh it's within this rivalry between the cosmopolitans and the and the nationalists, it's it belongs to the the latter, it seems. So uh how do you see this yeah impacting the thinking in the United States but is a close

partner to Iran and China should have had >> right yeah so right now um United States main adversary is not China um it's really Russia because Russia is the only country that has the resources uh biblical will um and the ter territorial integrity in order to challenge American hijgemony And so um right now America's major concern is is Russia and this war against Iran is really seen as a way to counter uh Russia's aggression in

Ukraine. Basically once Russia went to Ukraine um America has actually no choice but to um attack Iran in order to counter uh Russia's um possible control over the heartland. And so this is going to be the defining struggle for the world for next 20 years. Not between the United States and China but between Russia and America. And the great question is where will the world align over the next um 20 years? If history is a guide, most countries will start to line with Russia because if you go back

go back to the Pelisional War, the main aggressor was Athens. And what happened ultimately was that um the entire world ultimately aligned against Athens because Athens was seen as the great aggressor. So right now uh the great aggressor is America and we can see that the world especially Europe um and Iran and China turning to Russia as the great salvation and um what Russia will do is start to challenge America in the seas. Again I don't think there's any hope

that Russia will win against America and the seas. Uh but Russia has absolutely no choice but to start to challenge America.

item.title: Seyed Marandi 12 04 06 update on Iran Negotiations
item.content:encoded:
YouTube: https://www.youtube.com/shorts/DQHL5cOZg48

Transcript

mistakenly thinks that, uh, he can work wonders and that Iran will be humiliated or that he'll defeat Iran overnight with his overwhelming force or that if he destroys Iran's electrical power plants or whatever that Iranians will come uh, and submit. It's not going to happen. We saw what happened during the last 40 days. It was exactly as we had predicted. And this is something that you have been you and I have been talking about for a very, very long time now. Since the beginning when we've been I had the pleasure of being on your show.

I think that Trump is, uh, is going to cause great harm to the global economy unless, of course, something happens where he backs off, which at the moment because he is surrounded by Israeli pursuers, I don't see that happening because their priorities are not the United States. Their priorities are not the global economy. They're not India or Brazil or uh, South Africa. They're the Israeli regime.
